questions.rubric: Full credit: student chooses a single-core design for a small wearable or similarly constrained device and justifies it using predictability, simpler debugging, lower synchronization overhead, lower software complexity, or power efficiency. Accept answers that compare it against multicore or heterogeneous options.

Full credit: student explains that putting a timing-critical task and a heavy background task on the same core, or sharing data inefficiently across cores, can create contention, interference, jitter, latency, or missed deadlines. Accept answers that mention moving the heavy task to the other core or isolating work.

Full credit: student correctly compares Harvard and von Neumann architectures, especially the difference between separate instruction/data paths and shared paths. Accept discussion of reduced contention, better throughput, or modified Harvard behavior.

Full credit: student explains that a designer might choose a CISC core when compatibility with legacy x86 software, desktop-class operating systems, or a mature software ecosystem matters more than embedded-style simplicity, power, or timing predictability.
Full credit: student explains that big.LITTLE combines performance-oriented and efficiency-oriented cores to balance speed and power across mixed workloads.

keywords: instructions and data use separate paths
questions.rubric: MCQ: full credit if the submitted answer text identifies Harvard architecture as using separate instruction and data paths.

keywords: improve concurrency but add synchronization and debug complexity
questions.rubric: MCQ: full credit if the submitted answer text identifies the main multicore tradeoff as improved concurrency with added synchronization/debug complexity.

keywords: different safety, timing, or reliability requirements
questions.rubric: MCQ: full credit if the submitted answer text correctly defines mixed criticality as tasks with different timing, safety, or reliability requirements sharing a system.

keywords: heterogeneous architecture with performance and efficiency cores
questions.rubric: MCQ: full credit if the submitted answer text identifies big.LITTLE as a heterogeneous architecture combining performance and efficiency cores.
True/false: full credit if the student answers False to the statement that Harvard architecture always means two completely separate physical memories.

Auto-graded hardware telemetry. Expected format should include a timing tag and fields such as produced, consumed, missed, and status=PASS. For now, this server-side keyword grader checks only that the telemetry string includes all required fields. Example acceptable format: [timing:produced=20 consumed=20 missed=0 status=PASS].

Full credit: student explains that poor sharing or poor task placement can create contention, latency, jitter, or missed events, and that isolating work or moving heavy processing away from the timing-critical path improves responsiveness.
